behaviors.description: The generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) specified in the description.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: correctness depends entirely on what the real LLM generates — a mocked LLM returning a valid signature would always pass, hiding real failures where the model generates invalid Python or violates the interface spec. MOCK-HIDE: mocking the LLM to return a fixed valid signature masks the risk that the real model produces broken code. The failure boundary is crossed when the real LLM is called.
behaviors.test_description: Call the service with a function description that specifies exact parameter names, types, and return type. Use a real LLM to generate the implementation. Parse and extract the function signature from the output, then verify it matches the specification in the original description (names, types, and arity must all align).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Testing the parsing logic alone would only be Unit; testing that the LLM actually generates a correct signature requires Agentic.

behaviors.description: The generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: the behavior explicitly includes 'subsequent attempt', meaning the generate-test-refine-test loop is part of success. LLM-DEP: the code quality depends on real LLM output. REAL-INFRA: must actually execute the generated code against a real test suite to know if it passes. MOCK-HIDE: a mocked LLM configured to return passing code on attempt 2 would hide real failures where the model can't improve its own code.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a function description and a complete test suite. Call the service with a real LLM, allow it to run the full generate-test-refine loop for up to 3 attempts. Execute each generated function against the real test suite. Verify that within 3 iterations, all tests pass.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Testing just the first attempt would be Agentic; testing the refinement loop and whether it actually succeeds requires Workflow.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from its mistakes.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is 'does the service pass test failure messages to the next LLM call'—that's code logic, not LLM behavior. MOCK-MISS: a mocked LLM can record which prompts it received, so we can verify the failure message is present without needing a real LLM. However, REAL-INFRA: to trigger the failure path, we need at least one real test failure, which requires actually running the test suite against generated code. We can mock the first LLM to return code that definitely fails, then verify the failure output is in the next prompt.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return code that fails a test case on the first call. Execute the test suite (real) to generate a failure message. Verify that the failure message appears in the next LLM prompt. You can mock the second LLM call and assert the failure details are present in its input prompt.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Testing only the prompt-building logic in isolation would be Unit; including real test execution raises it to Integration.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error with all three attempts and their test results.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: this behavior is purely control-flow—stopping after N attempts is deterministic and doesn't depend on real LLM output. MOCK-HIDE: mocking the LLM and test runner doesn't hide the failure; the loop counter and error response structure are testable with mocks. REAL-INFRA: no real I/O needed—the service must have a retry counter that stops at 3 and collects results into a response object. Both are internal logic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to always return code that fails, mock the test suite to always fail, call the service, and verify it stops after exactly 3 iterations. Assert the returned error response contains all 3 attempts with their test results. Check that a 4th attempt is not made.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This is deterministic loop logic; mocks are sufficient.

behaviors.description: The full loop—generate, test, refine, test again—produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: the entire test hinges on whether the real LLM can improve its own code based on test feedback. MOCK-HIDE: a mocked LLM configured to return 'working code' on attempt 2 would always pass, even if the real model can't self-improve. MULTI-STEP: requires multiple real agentic calls in sequence—generate, test (fail), refine with feedback, test (pass). REAL-INFRA: must actually run generated code and test suite to verify the loop produces a working solution.
behaviors.test_description: Select a function that is known to be difficult for LLMs on first attempt (e.g., a non-trivial algorithm like edit distance, topological sort, or a tricky string manipulation). Provide the description and test suite to the service. Use a real LLM for all calls. Run the full generate-test-refine loop and verify that within 3 attempts, the generated function passes all tests.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Testing a simple function the LLM usually gets right on the first try would not test the refinement loop's value; selecting a hard function ensures we exercise the full Workflow.
